g.data-narration: Welcome to Merge Sort, an efficient O(n log n) divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm invented by John von Neumann in 1945.
g.text: BCS401 - Analysis and Design of Algorithms

g.data-narration: Merge Sort guarantees consistent O(n log n) performance for best, average, and worst cases, unlike Quick Sort which can degrade.
Merge Sort uses divide-and-conquer: split the array in half, sort each half recursively, then merge sorted results.
The MERGE_SORT function uses recursion with parameters A (array), p (start), q (middle), and r (end index).
The MERGE function combines two sorted subarrays A[p..q] and A[q+1..r] into one sorted array using sentinel values.

rect.data-narration: The MERGE function combines two sorted subarrays using sentinel ∞ values to simplify boundary checks during comparison.
rect.text: The MERGE Function - Heart of Merge Sort
Then compare and copy smallest to A[k]

g.data-narration: Let's trace Merge Sort on array [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]. First, we divide the array into two halves at the middle.
Continue dividing each half recursively until each subarray contains a single element, which is trivially sorted.
Now we merge: compare elements from two subarrays and place the smaller one first. [38,27] merges to [27,38].
g.text: Single elements merge into sorted pairs

g.data-narration: Continue merging sorted pairs: [27,38] and [3,43] merge to [3,27,38,43]. Similarly for the right half.
Final merge combines [3,27,38,43] and [9,10,82] to produce the completely sorted array [3,9,10,27,38,43,82].
g.text: Phase 3: Final Merge - Complete Sorted Array

g.data-narration: Time complexity is derived from recurrence: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n). Two subproblems of half size plus merge cost.
Using Master Theorem: a=2, b=2, f(n)=n. Since n^log₂² = n¹ = n, we have Case 2 giving Θ(n log n).
Merge Sort requires O(n) auxiliary space for temporary arrays during merging and is stable, preserving equal element order.
g.text: Auxiliary space for L[] and R[] arrays
O(n) space vs O(n log n) guarantee

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Θ(n) solves to Θ(n log n). Merge Sort is stable with O(n) space.
Exam tips: Remember the recurrence relation, master theorem, O(n) space, and stability. Don't confuse with Quick Sort's pivot approach.
g.text: Base case: single element is sorted
